macro_segments.title: The Status of Maamar According to Beit Shammai: Full Acquisition vs. Mere Deferment

micro_segments.title: Setting up the Question: Does Maamar Constitute a Complete Kinyan?
Rabbi Elazar's Position: Maamar Works Only to Push Off the Co-wife
Rav Avin's Proof from the Mishna: Why Maamar Cannot Solve the Achot Zikukato Problem
Rav Ashi's Alternative: Maamar Leaves a Remnant (Meshayer) Even with the Wife
Objection from the Mishna: How Can Leah Walk Away if Maamar is Only Meshayer?
Resolution: Yevama Who Could Have Had Yibum vs. Those Who Could Not

macro_segments.title: How Complete Is Maamar? Further Exploration of Kinyan Gamur, Eirusin, and Nisuin

micro_segments.title: Rava's Question: Does Maamar Accomplish Nisuin (Full Marriage) or Only Eirusin (Betrothal)?
Abaye's Response: Distinguishing Rights and Status from the Need for Chuppah
The Nature of Regular Eirusin: Lacks Inheritance, Tumah, and Vow Annulment
Rava Refines: Does Maamar Require an Actual Act of Chuppah Afterwards?
Resolution Through Non-Consensual Bi'ah: Sex Itself Constitutes Chuppah
Maamar Removes Zikat Yevamim: Different Track from Yibum

macro_segments.title: Vow Annulment (Hafarat Nedarim) as Evidence of Maamar's Status

micro_segments.title: Shomeret Yavam: Differing Opinions on Who Can Annul Vows of a Sister-in-Law
Rabbi Eliezer vs. Rabbi Yehoshua vs. Rabbi Akiva: Strength of Zikat Yevamim
Rav Ami bar Abba: The Case Involves Maamar, Not Mere Shomeret Yavam
If Maamar Creates Nisuin Status, Vow Annulment Works Alone; If Only Eirusin, Father's Participation Required
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak's Resolution: Vows Annulled in Partnership with Father
Application to Rav Elazar's View: How Can Vow Annulment Work if Maamar is Only Meshayer?
The Court Case Scenario: Woman Sues for Support, Vows Assumed Under Husband's Implicit Consent

macro_segments.title: Three Scenarios with Siblings Married to Two Sisters Plus an Outsider: Death and Yibum Sequencing

micro_segments.title: Case One: Reuven (Married to Rachel) Dies; Levi Does Yibum with Leah; Then Levi Dies; Shimon's Options
Result: Rachel Walks Away (Wife's Sister), Devorah Requires Chalitzah (Quasi Tzarat Erva)
Rav Nachman: No Zikat Yevamim (Ein Zikat Yevamim Afilu B'Echad Acha)
Case Two: Levi Dies First; Reuven Does Yibum with Devorah; Then Reuven Dies; Shimon's Options
Result: Rachel Walks Away (Wife's Sister), Devorah Requires Chalitzah (Real Wife, Quasi Connection)
Gemara: Case 1 More Obvious; Case 2 Shows Kal Va-Chomer; Order of Teaching Explained

macro_segments.title: Case Three: Sequential Deaths Including Leah Before Final Yibum Opportunity – Status Lock at Time of Death

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Reuven Dies; Before Levi Acts, Leah Dies; Levi Then Dies; Can Shimon Marry Rachel?
Mishna's Answer: Rachel Remains Forbidden Forever (Asurah Olamit) Because She Was Forbidden at the Hour of Death
Rav Yehuda: All Sister-in-Law Not Called Yevama at Time of Death Remains as Eshes Ach
Gemara: This Is Exactly What the Mishna Says; Why Attribute to Rav Yehuda?
Distinction: Single Brother Case vs. Multiple Brothers – Status Complete vs. In Process
Proof That Status at Death Locks: Another Mishna Where Death Changes Status Before Yibum, Yet Remains Forbidden

macro_segments.title: Case Four: Divorce Rather Than Death – Divorced Sister-in-Law Frees the Rival Wife for Yibum

micro_segments.title: Scenario: Shimon Divorces Leah (Not Death); Levi Dies; Shimon Can Now Do Yibum with Devorah
Principle: Achot Ishto Forbidding Applies Only While Woman Lives; Divorce Has Same Effect
Mishna's Conclusion: All Died or All Divorced – Rival Wives Are Permitted

Rav Yehuda (rule on status lock at time of death)
Rav Ami bar Abba (identifies maamar context in vow case)
Rav Nachman bar Yitzchak (partnership vow annulment solution)
Rav Pinchas (woman's vows done under husband's implied consent)
Achot Isha (wife's sister as forbidden relative)
Status lock at time of death (She'ah Achat)
Rav Elazar's threefold distinction (complete, pushes off, leaves remnant)
